summary: I was inducted into the service on July 7th, 1941, which was five months before Pearl Harbor and well before D-Day. Although I was in the Army and later the Air Force during the war, I didn't directly participate in D-Day. However, I was aware of the major events unfolding in the war, including D-Day, as I trained and prepared for overseas deployment.

I was drafted into the Army five months before Pearl Harbor was attacked, so I was already in service when that happened. Pearl Harbor was a pivotal moment that made the war very real for me and my family. It confirmed my feeling that the U.S. would be pulled into the war, and it changed the nature of my training and deployment.

I was born and raised in Chicago and worked at a drug company there before being drafted. I went to Crane Tech High School on the West Side of Chicago and graduated in 1936. Chicago was my home base before I entered the military and started my training across the country.

When I was first drafted, I was supposed to go to Fort Riley, Kansas, which was a cavalry horse outfit at the time. Since I had no experience with horses and wanted to be in the Air Force, I got discharged from the cavalry and enlisted in the Air Corps instead. Fort Riley was the place that started me on the path away from cavalry and toward aviation.

I enlisted in the Air Force after being discharged from the cavalry, believing it was a better branch. I trained as an airplane mechanic and later as a gunner, serving with the 8th Air Force during World War II. The Air Force was my military home throughout the war, and I flew many missions with them over Europe.

I attended Crane Tech High School on the West Side of Chicago after junior high was closed. I graduated from Crane in 1936. It was the school where I completed my formal education before entering the workforce and eventually the military.

After enlisting in the Air Force, I was sent to Fort Sheridan for initial processing before being sent to Jefferson Barracks for basic training. Fort Sheridan was one of the first military bases I experienced as a new recruit. It marked the beginning of my formal military journey.

I went to Jefferson Barracks in Saint Louis for basic training after Fort Sheridan. The training there was pretty minimal, mostly marching between tents and the mess hall for six weeks. It was early in the war, so the Air Force was still ramping up its training programs.

I was sent to Saint Louis for basic training at Jefferson Barracks. It was my first real military experience after enlisting in the Air Force. The city was a stop on my path through various training bases across the country.

After basic training, I was sent to Chinook Field near Champaign, Illinois, for airplane mechanic school. We completed our schooling there around January or February of 1942. Chinook Field was where I gained technical skills that prepared me for my later roles in the Air Force.

Chinook Field, where I trained as an airplane mechanic, is located near Champaign, Illinois. I spent part of early 1942 in that area completing my technical training. It was an important step before I moved on to other bases for further training and assignments.

After mechanic school, I was sent to Greenville, Mississippi, which was a basic training base for cadets. I became an instructor there around August 1942. Greenville was where I met my wife, who came down to be with me while I was stationed there.

Later, I was transferred to Greenwood, Mississippi, where I worked as a mechanic on the cable controls of B-13A planes. It was during this time that I volunteered for gunnery school. Greenwood was a key place in my transition from mechanic to aircrew member.

While stationed in Greenwood, Mississippi, I worked on the cable controls of B-13A planes. These aircraft were part of my early Air Force experience before I became a gunner. The B-13 was a significant part of my mechanic duties during that period.

In June, I was sent to Fort Myers, Florida, for six weeks of gunnery school. This training was exciting because it promised wings, extra pay, and stripes. Fort Myers was where I officially transitioned into a combat aircrew role.

After gunnery school, I was sent to Salt Lake City, Utah, for further training and assignment to B-24 or B-17 crews. Salt Lake City was one of several stops as I moved closer to deployment overseas. It was part of the process of forming and training my crew.

From Salt Lake City, I went to Boise, Idaho, where I was put on a crew that I stayed with through the rest of training. Boise was an important place for bonding with my crew and preparing for combat missions. It was part of the final stages of our stateside training.

We moved on to Casper, Wyoming, for phase training as part of the Cecil Isbell Group, led by Colonel Isbell, a former football star. My wife came out to be with me during this time. Casper was where we polished our skills before heading overseas.

While training in Casper, Wyoming, I was part of the Cecil Isbell Group, named after Colonel Cecil Isbell, who was a big football player with the Army. He led our group during this phase of training. His leadership was a notable part of our preparation before deployment.

After finishing training, my crew was sent to Topeka, Kansas, to pick up our plane for overseas deployment. Unfortunately, the base only had B-17s, so our crew was split between two planes and flown overseas by the Army transport command. Topeka was the last stop before heading to Europe.

Our route overseas took us through Syracuse, New York, as part of the journey to Europe. It was one of several stops including Presque Isle and Gander Lake before crossing the Atlantic. Syracuse was a key waypoint in our transit to the war front.

Presque Isle, Maine, was another stop on our way to Europe after leaving Syracuse. From there, we went to Gander Lake in Newfoundland before making the transatlantic flight. Presque Isle was part of the chain of bases used for ferrying crews overseas.

Gander Lake in Newfoundland was the last stop in North America before we flew across the Atlantic to Scotland. It was a critical refueling and staging point for many crews heading to the European theater. We landed in Prestwick, Scotland, shortly after leaving Gander Lake.

We landed in Prestwick, Scotland, just before Thanksgiving of 1943. It was our first arrival in a foreign country and the start of our combat deployment. From Prestwick, we were sent to Tring, near London, to join operational units.

From Prestwick, we were sent to Tring, about an hour north of London, where we were replacement crews for various bases. Our crew was assigned to the 448th Bomb Group after replacing a crew that had been shot down. Tring was where we integrated into active combat units.

Tring, where we were stationed for a time, was about an hour's train ride north of London. Being so close to London was a constant reminder of the war going on in England and Europe. London was the major city near our base and part of the backdrop of our deployment.

Our crew was assigned to the 448th Bomb Group after arriving in England. We replaced a crew that had been shot down and flew our first mission on February 5th, 1944. The 448th was part of the 8th Air Force and conducted strategic bombing missions over Europe.

On our first mission, our plane was attacked by a Focke-Wulf Fw 190 fighter. It hit the wing position of our plane, the Mary Michelle, which was later rebuilt and flown on another mission. The Fw 190 was a formidable enemy aircraft we faced during combat.

The Mary Michelle was the plane we flew on our first mission after it was rebuilt from damage sustained in combat. It was a B-17 that burned too much gas and was eventually taken out of service. Flying the Mary Michelle was a memorable part of my combat experience.

I served with the 8th Air Force, specifically the 184th Bomb Group, which was part of the strategic bombing campaign over Germany. We flew many missions targeting airfields, factories, and other military assets. The 8th Air Force was known for its heavy bombing raids and was a major part of the air war in Europe.

I was part of the 184th Bomb Group within the 8th Air Force. Our group flew numerous missions over Germany and occupied Europe, targeting key military and industrial sites. The 184th was a tight-knit unit, and my crew was lucky to complete our tour without casualties.

We flew a mission targeting the ski sites or launching pads of the V-2 rockets. On that mission, our lead ship was shot down, and we had to abort the bomb run and return to base with our bombs. The V-2 rocket sites were high-value targets during the war.

Although I did not directly mention James H. Doolittle in my story, he was a famous figure in the Air Force during World War II. My service with the 8th Air Force was part of the broader air war that leaders like Doolittle helped shape. His legacy was part of the Air Force culture during my time.

entity: Surgeon General of the United States
summary: The Surgeon General of the United States was responsible for medical policies during the war, including the use of escape kits and medical supplies carried on our planes. We had morphine and other medications in our kits in case of injury. The medical support was crucial for aircrews like mine during combat missions.
